name: Florida Dept of Children & Families (DCF)
tagline: Florida's human-services backbone: DCF runs child welfare, mental health and substance-abuse services, and the ACCESS Florida public-benefits eligibility engine — a data-heavy agency in the middle of a multi-year child-welfare system modernization.
overview: The Florida Department of Children and Families (DCF) is the state's primary human-services agency, responsible for child welfare (abuse and neglect intake, investigations, and case management), mental health and substance-abuse services, and public-benefits eligibility through the ACCESS Florida program (SNAP, Temporary Cash Assistance, and Medicaid eligibility determination). It is one of Florida's most data-intensive agencies: it operates statewide eligibility and case-management systems, the MyACCESS self-service portal, and the Florida Safe Families Network (FSFN) child-welfare case system. DCF processes very high transaction volumes — by its own public figures it handles, on average, hundreds of thousands of Medicaid applications, redeterminations, and assistance requests each month. The agency is in the middle of a multi-year modernization to a federally compliant CCWIS (Comprehensive Child Welfare Information System), making it an active modernization account where data integration, cloud, and security work all surface. For a Dell SLG seller, DCF is a large, infrastructure- and modernization-driven buyer reached primarily through Florida's central procurement vehicles rather than agency-only RFPs.

budget.intro: DCF is a large direct spender funded through Florida's annual General Appropriations Act, with a substantial federal match flowing through SNAP, Medicaid eligibility, and Title IV-E child-welfare funding. Governor DeSantis signed the $117.6B FY2026–27 budget on June 29, 2026 (after ~$1.6B in vetoes); the Governor's proposal had earmarked more than $4.8B for the Department. Confirm DCF's final line items in the enacted General Appropriations Act — including any technology appropriations and vetoes — before forecasting.

stats.label: FY2026–27 (proposed)
stats.note: Governor's proposed DCF figure; the $117.6B state budget was signed June 29, 2026 — confirm DCF's enacted line items and any vetoes

stats.label: Eligibility transactions
stats.note: DCF-cited monthly average of Medicaid applications, redeterminations, and assistance requests

stats.label: CCWIS modernization
stats.note: Phased child-welfare system rebuild with IV&V oversight

notes: DCF's appropriation mixes state general revenue with large federal streams (SNAP/Medicaid administration, Title IV-E) — funding for IT and modernization often rides federal cost-allocation rules.
SNAP caseloads fell sharply across Florida in 2025–2026 following federal program changes (H.R. 1); shifting caseloads affect eligibility-system load and modernization priorities.

The FY2026–27 state budget was signed June 29, 2026; the $4.8B+ cited here is the Governor's earlier proposed DCF number — confirm the enacted General Appropriations Act line items and any DCF vetoes before committing to a forecast.

initiatives.name: CCWIS child-welfare modernization
initiatives.summary: DCF is replacing/modernizing its child-welfare technology toward a federally compliant CCWIS, in a modular, phased approach starting with intake, investigations, and case management, and building new enterprise-architecture foundations. Independent IV&V oversight is in place. A multi-year program — strong surface for data, cloud, and integration infrastructure.

initiatives.name: ACCESS Florida / MyACCESS eligibility
initiatives.summary: The ACCESS Florida eligibility engine and the MyACCESS self-service portal determine SNAP, cash-assistance, and Medicaid eligibility at very high volume. Reliability, capacity, and customer-facing digital experience are recurring priorities — an infrastructure and benefits-modernization play.

initiatives.name: Data integration & cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: DCF holds highly sensitive child-welfare, behavioral-health, and benefits data and shares it across agencies (e.g., Medicaid, juvenile justice). Data integration, identity, and security controls are persistent requirements alongside the CCWIS rebuild.

initiatives.name: Behavioral-health capacity
initiatives.summary: Recent budgets emphasize expanding mental-health and substance-abuse capacity and modernizing the continuum of care. Supporting systems, data, and reporting follow these program expansions.

procurement.intro: DCF buys through Florida's centralized procurement framework, administered by the Department of Management Services (DMS). Confirm the right vehicle before pursuing a deal: vendors must register in the Vendor Information Portal, and most commodity IT flows through State Term Contracts and MyFloridaMarketplace; larger or specialized programs (like CCWIS) run as dedicated solicitations.

vehicles.name: DMS State Term Contracts
vehicles.detail: Pre-competed statewide contracts administered by the Department of Management Services for commodity hardware, software, and services — the default path for routine IT buys by agencies including DCF.

vehicles.name: MyFloridaMarketplace (MFMP)
vehicles.detail: Florida's eProcurement and purchase-order/invoicing system. Vendors must register in the Vendor Information Portal (VIP) to do business with the state; agencies transact through MFMP.

vehicles.name: Agency / program solicitations
vehicles.detail: Large or specialized programs — notably CCWIS modernization and eligibility systems — run as their own competitive solicitations, often with separate IV&V oversight contracts.

powerStructure.name: Taylor N. Hatch
powerStructure.title: Secretary, DCF — resigning (June 2026)
powerStructure.note: Confirmed by the full Senate 33-4 in March 2026, but announced her resignation on ~June 30, 2026; no successor named as of this update and her last day was not confirmed by the Governor's office. Treat the top of the DCF account as in transition and confirm the new Secretary (likely an interim/acting leader first) before engaging.

powerStructure.name: Kathryn 'Kate' Williams
powerStructure.title: Deputy Secretary
powerStructure.note: Operational and policy leadership across child & family well-being, economic self-sufficiency, behavioral health, and quality/innovation — a key cross-program decision-maker.

powerStructure.name: Bridget Royster
powerStructure.title: Assistant Secretary, Economic Self-Sufficiency (ESS)
powerStructure.note: Owns the ACCESS Florida / MyACCESS benefits-eligibility program — the buying center for eligibility-system and benefits-modernization work.

powerStructure.name: Office of Information Technology / CIO
powerStructure.title: DCF technology leadership
powerStructure.note: Owns DCF's systems including FSFN and the CCWIS modernization; the agency CIO role has shown recent turnover, so confirm the current officeholder before engaging.

news.title: DCF Secretary Taylor Hatch announces resignation; no successor named
news.impact: The top of the DCF account is now in transition just months after her confirmation — expect an interim/acting leader and a possible reset of modernization and budget priorities. Confirm the new Secretary before engaging at the executive level.

news.title: Gov. DeSantis signs $117.6B FY2026–27 budget (June 29) after ~$1.6B in vetoes
news.impact: The enacted budget sets DCF's actual FY2026–27 funding; the Governor had proposed $4.8B+ for the Department. Confirm DCF's final technology line items and any vetoes before forecasting.

news.title: Florida Senate confirms Taylor Hatch as DCF Secretary (33-4)
news.impact: Ended two years of unconfirmed status, but the tenure proved short-lived given her June resignation — underscoring leadership volatility at the agency.

news.title: Governor's 'Floridians First' FY2026–27 budget proposes $4.8B+ for DCF
news.impact: Signaled continued investment in modernizing the continuum of care and behavioral-health capacity; now superseded by the enacted budget — confirm final line items.

news.title: Florida SNAP caseloads fall to multi-year lows after federal program changes
news.impact: Shifting eligibility volumes affect ACCESS Florida system load and may reshape benefits-modernization priorities.

news.title: DCF reflects on 2025: CCWIS child-welfare modernization advances toward implementation
news.impact: The multi-year system rebuild is an active modernization program — a recurring surface for data, cloud, and integration infrastructure.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's coverage on DMS State Term Contracts and active VIP/MyFloridaMarketplace registration — these are the access path for routine DCF IT buys.
Engage the CCWIS modernization as the lead motion: position data-platform, storage, and integration infrastructure plus security around the phased child-welfare rebuild.
Tie ACCESS Florida / MyACCESS capacity and reliability to compute, storage, and cloud infrastructure — a benefits-modernization play at very high transaction volume.
Lead with cybersecurity and data protection given DCF's highly sensitive child-welfare, behavioral-health, and benefits data and cross-agency data sharing.

Watch the leadership transition: Secretary Hatch is resigning (June 2026) with no named successor — anchor near-term relationships on the stable layer (Deputy Secretary Williams, ESS Assistant Secretary Royster, and the CIO/IT office) and confirm the new/acting Secretary and current CIO before forecasting.
